Objectives
1. To conduct a scoping review to map out primary health care delivered to PLWHIV.
2. Using a database of electronic health records in general practice, examine the proportion of PLWHIV who received
primary health care and if this varied among different regions, demographic and socioeconomic groups
3. To work with key partners including PLWHIV, HIV specialists and GPs, to co-design an intervention to optimise primary

public engagement including with PLWHIV.

are members of BHIVA external relations committee and will be involved in in disseminating research findings through their
networks including conferences.
RM, WL, JVR, ANa, THI, JN, PB, JC and HC will also form part of the stakeholder group in addition to recruited members.
Project Plan (including Patient and Public Involvement)
Stakeholder involvement using Person-Based Approach (led by ANa/THI/ANe/SD)
We will use a Person-Based Approach (PBA) throughout all three workstreams to engage with PLWHIV to develop a care model
that benefits them (see diagram).(27) Two co-applicants (ANa,THI) are PLWHIV and work in the HIV charity sector; their
involvement will ensure PLWHIV influence decision-making at all levels throughout the study.
The PBA offers practical and systematic methods of carrying out many of the key intervention development activities
recommended by the Medical Research Council (MRC) funded INDEX guidance for identifying and assessing approaches to
developing complex interventions.(28) It has been used to design interventions that demonstrated effectiveness in randomised
t

underserved groups.
Within 18 months, we will have a model of care ready for piloting locally in Bristol, North Somerset and South Gloucester
(BNSSG) area. We aim to secure a further grant (e.g. NIHR Programme Grant for Applied Research) for a feasibility study over
24 months. This model will be further developed after evaluation to be tested for effectiveness, cost-effectiveness and
appropriateness in a cluster randomised trial across the UK over 36 months. We aim to use the PBA process and have the same
stakeholders to give it continuity.
Our dissemination strategy will target professionals, policy makers and the public. We will publish outputs in high impact journals
and present at conferences. We will work with our PPI and communications team to write news stories to share via social media
and write press releases e.g. for newspapers and TV, and to engage with PLWHIV via peer networks.
We hope this project will create impact to improve the health and standardise the care of PLWHIV at scale, using a model that is
acceptable to PLWHIV, HIV specialists and GPs, that would eventually be adopted in clinical guidelines.

evidence.text:
Plain English summary of Reserarch
Aim(s) of the Research
We want to develop a way in which people living with HIV (PLWHIV) can see their general practitioners (GPs) regularly for health
checks and help them manage their HIV and other conditions to keep them healthy as they get older.
Background to the Research
PLWHIV now have a near-normal lifespan due to effective treatment for HIV. There are now double the number of PLWHIV over
the age of 50 than there were 10 years ago. They are more likely to develop mental health issues, heart problems, diabetes and
kidney problems compared with people without HIV. This is why they need extra health checks and care from their GPs.
There isn't much guidance on what GPs should do to support PLWHIV, nor what works best. Not everyone tells their GP about
their HIV diagnosis nor sees them due to stigma and past poor experiences with healthcare.
Design and Methods Used
The research uses the Person-Based Approach, where the input of PLWHIV is central to the planning and carrying out the
research. We aim to develop better ways of caring for PLWHIV that reflects what is wanted and needed by patients and care
providers.
There are three parts to our res

evidence.text:
earch:
1. We will review recent studies on different models of care (ways of providing healthcare) and get feedback from PLWHIV.
2. We will use a database of anonymised GP records to examine if PLWHIV are getting health checks from their GPs.
3. Using the results of the review of studies, a steering group (made up of PLWHIV, GPs, HIV specialists, and researchers)
will design an initial model of care for PLWHIV. We will hold focus group with other PLWHIV and health care
professionals to get their feedback on the model we have developed.
The steering group will make changes to the model from feedback. A revised model will be tested in "the real world" in a future
study. The findings will provide evidence for clinical guidance to improve the health care of PLWHIV so that they can live longer
and healthier lives.
Patient and Public Involvement (PPI)
Two people with lived experience of HIV have helped to write the research proposal and will be part of the study team throughout
the project. They will help design study materials, co-lead the steering group, review results, write and help share research
findings.
Dissemination
When we have the results of the study, we will share them with

evidence.text:
PLWHIV, there is little clinical
guidance on what this might look like. The British HIV Association (BHIVA) published a scoping review in 2017 on different care
models (ways of delivering care) to PLWHIV.(22) It identified three models used to manage long-term conditions that might be
relevant to HIV: current standard specialist model, shared-care and collaborative-care models.
In the current standard model, the HIV clinic manages all issues related to HIV, and any non-HIV conditions are referred back to
GPs. A shared-care model has agreed care protocols between partners to support the co-ordination of care.(23) There were few
examples where GPs managed most aspects of HIV care including drug monitoring, but the quality of evidence was poor; there
were no consistent association with improved clinical outcomes, cost effectiveness or acceptability.(24) Collaborative-care models
originated from mental health services and use case-based management and structured care plans shared with everyone
involved in the care of the individual. While they are more fluid and responsive, they require resources to support communication
to all health and social care providers. For a very long-term co

ndition such as HIV, it needs to change focus for different support
services at different stages of an individual’s HIV journey.(22)
For PLWHIV, there are barriers to receiving care from GPs. Poor experiences, whether real or perceived, might make it less likely
for PLWHIV to share their diagnosis and attend check-ups. A systematic review reported common factors that are key to
improving PLWHIV’s experiences of GP care include: perceived competence, communication and continuity.(25)
Before we explore what a good care model might look like, we need to conduct an up-to-date scoping review to map out current
practices as some research are now dated. This review needs to expand on the previous BHIVA report to include PLWHIV’s
experiences of general practice to examine barriers and facilitators to access, what care is already being provided by GPs to
PLWHIV, and what the gaps might be. An NIHR-funded feasibility study of a geriatric assessment for PLWHIV in hospital settings
is underway.(26)
Research question / aims and objectives
Aims and objectives
To develop a model of care to optimise primary health care for PLWHIV in UK general practice

report the results; and consult with stakeholders to inform or validate study finding.(32)
We will conduct a search of published and grey literature using MEDLINE, EMBASE, NICE Evidence, the Cochrane
Collaboration, Google and specialist websites (including, Aidsmap, and World Health Organization), and trial registries such as
the International Standard Randomised Controlled Trial Number (ISRCTN). The review will be summarised as: evaluations of
care models delivered in primary care for PLWHIV, ethnographic summaries of experiences of primary care and scope of primary
health care delivered in specialist settings. The findings will inform the co-design of an intervention for Workstream 3.

We will conduct a search of published and grey literature using MEDLINE, EMBASE, NICE Evidence, the Cochrane
Collaboration, Google and specialist websites (including, Aidsmap, and World Health Organization), and trial registries such as
the International Standard Randomised Controlled Trial Number (ISRCTN). The review will be summarised as: evaluations of
care models delivered in primary care for PLWHIV, ethnographic summaries of experiences of primary care and scope of primary
health care delivered in specialist settings. The findings will inform the co-design of an intervention for Workstream 3.
To save research costs and improve efficiency, there is capacity to make a start on this workstream at an early stage.

evidence.text:
ventative care (e.g. health
promotion, disease screening and vaccination). At least 96% of the UK population are registered with a general practitioner (GP),
and on average, consult their GP five times a year.(13, 14) Multimorbidity (more than two diseases in one person) is common in
the general population; one study reported 58% of patients attending general practices had multimorbidity, but they accounted for
78% of all consultations.(15)
HIV clinic staff might not have the expertise to deliver care to an ageing cohort of PLWHIV with comorbidities, mental health
needs and frailty compared with GP teams. This project will explore the role of general practice in the long-term care for PLWHIV
to help them receive the right care, in the right place, that is right for them, and lead longer and healthier lives.
Why is this research important?
New HIV diagnoses in England rose by 22% from 3,118 in 2021 to 3,805 in 2022. There were 94,397 PLWHIV accessing care in
England in 2022, a rise of 3% from 91,368 in 2021. Due to successful management of HIV, the proportion of PLWHIV aged over
50 years have almost doubled in 10 years from 27% in 2012 to 50% in 2022. (16)
Over a quarter of adults i

n England are estimated to have multimorbidity, and is expected to double between 2015 and 2035.(17)
People with long-term conditions use more health services, accounting for 70% of the total health and social care spend in
England alone.(18) Despite this, levels of unmet need are high, particularly for support from community services to manage
multiple conditions.(19)
Investment in general practice to provide capacity and designing integrated healthcare around patient needs have been the
driving principles behind The NHS Five Year Forward View and The NHS Long Term Plan.(20, 21) This research proposal aligns
with these priorities by generating evidence on how general practice can contribute effectively to the long-term care of PLWHIV,
so that they benefit from longer and healthier lives, and health service resources are used effectively .
This study will contribute to evidence-based clinical guidance on the care of PLWHIV in general practice to be implemented within
five years of this project to benefit the health and care of an ageing cohort of PLWHIV.
Review of existing evidence
Despite the potential contribution of general practice to deliver long-term and preventative care for

underserved groups.
Within 18 months, we will have a model of care ready for piloting locally in Bristol, North Somerset and South Gloucester
(BNSSG) area. We aim to secure a further grant (e.g. NIHR Programme Grant for Applied Research) for a feasibility study over
24 months. This model will be further developed after evaluation to be tested for effectiveness, cost-effectiveness and
appropriateness in a cluster randomised trial across the UK over 36 months. We aim to use the PBA process and have the same
stakeholders to give it continuity.
Our dissemination strategy will target professionals, policy makers and the public. We will publish outputs in high impact journals
and present at conferences. We will work with our PPI and communications team to write news stories to share via social media
and write press releases e.g. for newspapers and TV, and to engage with PLWHIV via peer networks.
We hope this project will create impact to improve the health and standardise the care of PLWHIV at scale, using a model that is
acceptable to PLWHIV, HIV specialists and GPs, that would eventually be adopted in clinical guidelines.

that benefits them (see diagram).(27) Two co-applicants (ANa,THI) are PLWHIV and work in the HIV charity sector; their
involvement will ensure PLWHIV influence decision-making at all levels throughout the study.
The PBA offers practical and systematic methods of carrying out many of the key intervention development activities
recommended by the Medical Research Council (MRC) funded INDEX guidance for identifying and assessing approaches to
developing complex interventions.(28) It has been used to design interventions that demonstrated effectiveness in randomised
t

rials for reducing antibiotic prescribing in primary care. (29, 30) PBA supports meaningful patient and public involvement (PPI) in
research. The public and stakeholders are involved in the co-production of an intervention which improves its ownership,
applicability and acceptability in practice. The PBA process is transparent as the public and other stakeholders’ views are
systematically recorded, acknowledged and acted upon.

2. Are PLWHIV receiving primary health care from their GPs?
3. What does a “good” model of primary health care for PLWHIV look like?
Objectives
1. To conduct a scoping review to map out primary health care delivered to PLWHIV.
2. Using a database of electronic health records in general practice, examine the proportion of PLWHIV who received
primary health care and if this varied among different regions, demographic and socioeconomic groups
3. To work with key partners including PLWHIV, HIV specialists and GPs, to co-design an intervention to optimise primary
health care provision for PLWHIV.
Research Team
Our research team reflects a mix of clinical and research expertise; experience in advocacy, commissioning; and track record of
public engagement including with PLWHIV.

evidence.text:
underserved groups.
Within 18 months, we will have a model of care ready for piloting locally in Bristol, North Somerset and South Gloucester
(BNSSG) area. We aim to secure a further grant (e.g. NIHR Programme Grant for Applied Research) for a feasibility study over
24 months. This model will be further developed after evaluation to be tested for effectiveness, cost-effectiveness and
appropriateness in a cluster randomised trial across the UK over 36 months. We aim to use the PBA process and have the same
stakeholders to give it continuity.
Our dissemination strategy will target professionals, policy makers and the public. We will publish outputs in high impact journals
and present at conferences. We will work with our PPI and communications team to write news stories to share via social media
and write press releases e.g. for newspapers and TV, and to engage with PLWHIV via peer networks.
We hope this project will create impact to improve the health and standardise the care of PLWHIV at scale, using a model that is
acceptable to PLWHIV, HIV specialists and GPs, that would eventually be adopted in clinical guidelines.

Scoping review – months 2 to 9
We will identify up-to-date studies on models of care, care experiences, and map out primary health care delivered by HIV
services. These would contribute to further

evidence base and the co-design in workstream 3.
Analysis of primary care database - months 1 to 11
The findings will identify the engagement between PLWHIV and their GPs, and what more could be achievable and feasible for
general practice to deliver.
We will identify who isn’t accessing or receiving care from GPs. This might reflect the intersectionality of stigma from HIV, racism
and/or sexism. Discussions at the co-design phase will include suggestions to overcome such barriers.
Non-engagement could be interpreted differently. A healthy male PLWHIV with stable disease might receive regular
cardiovascular assessment in their HIV clinic, and annual influenza vaccinations from a pharmacist, so might not feel he needs
GP care. Further research using qualitative studies would help to understand this better.
Codesign of a model of care - months 1-11. Focus group months 11 to 13. Refinement: month 16.
We anticipate a model of care that would be appropriate and acceptable to all stakeholders. This might include education and
training for primary care providers to improve confidentiality, care navigation, improving two-way communication between GPs
and HIV clinics and better access for

3. What does a “good” model of primary health care for PLWHIV look like?
Objectives
1. To conduct a scoping review to map out primary health care delivered to PLWHIV.
2. Using a database of electronic health records in general practice, examine the proportion of PLWHIV who received
primary health care and if this varied among different regions, demographic and socioeconomic groups
3. To work with key partners including PLWHIV, HIV specialists and GPs, to co-design an intervention to optimise primary
health care provision for PLWHIV.
Research Team
Our research team reflects a mix of clinical and research expertise; experience in advocacy, commissioning; and track record of
public engagement including with PLWHIV.

Equality, diversity and inclusion
PLWHIV are not a homogenous group. Men who have sex with men and Black African people are disproportionately affected;
there were 212 trans and gender diverse people seen for HIV care in England in 2022. (16) The number of PLWHIV is higher in
metropolitan areas but those outside might have different needs. We will ensure PLWHIV in our stakeholder group not only
reflects the demographics most affected by HIV, but also those who might be underrepresented.
Workstream 1 –Scoping Review (months 2 to 9, led by RM/JH/JV/WL/SD/SRA)
Research question 1 - How is primary health care are delivered to PLWHIV?
We will conduct a scoping review to identify how primary health care, such as cardiovascular risk screening, vaccinations and
cervical cytology, are delivered to PLWHIV.
This scoping method is preferable to a systematic review because of the range of literature that needs to be explored
